questions.sentence: Merging and rebasing are two primary ways to integrate changes from one branch into another in Git.
Merging creates a new 'merge commit' that ties the histories of both branches together.
Rebasing, on the other hand, moves or 'replays' your branch's commits on top of another branch.
However, rebasing involves rewriting history and can be dangerous if used on shared public branches.
Most modern teams use a combination: rebasing local feature branches to keep history tidy, while using merge for official releases on the main branch.
